perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect, notwithstanding the completion of the Purpose, for a period of seven years from the Effective Date unless extended, superseded or otherwise varied by a subsequent written agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect, notwithstanding the completion of the Purpose, for a period of one year from the Effective Date unless extended, superseded or otherwise varied by a subsequent written agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The DTSA does not specify a duration, but the statute of limitations for bringing a claim under the DTSA is 3 years after the date of discovery of the misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could be seen as undermining the protection of trade secrets afforded by the DTSA, especially given the 3 year statute of limitations for DTSA claims. One year may be an unreasonably short amount of time to protect valuable confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from seven years to one year, which could be seen as insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially considering the DTSA's 3-year statute of limitations for misappropriation claims. The 'Law Explanation' and 'Perturbation Explanation' both suggest that this shorter period undermines the protection of trade secrets. The 'Scraped Law Snippet 2' references the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, establishing its relevance and applicability. Although the snippet does not explicitly state a required duration for trade secret protection, the potential conflict with the 3-year statute of limitations supports the determination that the modified contract term creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication or otherwise to any third p arty (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for carrying out the Purpose; b. make copies of the Information (or any fur ther information derived from the Information) in whatever form or medium only to the extent that the copies are reasonably necessary for the Purpose and clearly mark all such copies as confidential; c. take all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement; and d. at the request and direction of the Disclosing Party, and without delay, return or destroy any Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and any copies of such Information, except that one copy may be kept by the Receiving Party for archival purposes and for the purpose of defending itself against any claims arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication or otherwise to any third p arty (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for carrying out the Purpose; b. make copies of the Information (or any fur ther information derived from the Information) in whatever form or medium only to the extent that the copies are reasonably necessary for the Purpose and clearly mark all such copies as confidential; c. take only reasonable security precautions to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement; and d. at the request and direction of the Disclosing Party, and without delay, return or destroy any Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and any copies of such Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the security precaution standard to 'reasonable' from 'at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information' potentially violates data protection laws like GDPR if the Receiving Party's precautions are inadequate.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR mandates appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The modified text weakens the security obligations and could lead to a breach of GDPR if the 'reasonable' security measures are insufficient to protect personal data contained within the disclosed information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated security precautions 'at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information,' while the modified version only requires 'reasonable security precautions.' The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate that GDPR Article 32 requires 'appropriate technical and organizational measures' relative to the risk. By weakening the security obligation to merely 'reasonable', the modified text creates an inconsistency that could lead to a GDPR breach if those 'reasonable' measures are insufficient to protect personal data, thus creating a legal contradiction. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly contain GDPR article 32, the Law and Perturbation explanation reference it, supporting the determination of a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of such requirement or obligation (together with a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise to such belief) in advance of the required disclosure, to enable the Disclosing Party to seek appropriate protective relief and/or to take other steps to resist or narrow the scope of any required disclosure; Non-Disclosure Agreement Energy Technologies Institute © 2016 Energy Technologies Institute LLP.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. provide the Disclosing Party with written notice of such requirement or obligation (together with a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise to such belief) after the required disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'in advance of the required disclosure' to 'after the required disclosure' contradicts the intent of many data protection laws which require notification and an opportunity to object before disclosure, particularly if the information contains personal data governed by GDPR.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 14(5) of GDPR requires informing the data subject of the disclosure, and this modification prevents the disclosing party from taking steps to comply with GDPR before the disclosure occurs. Deferring notification until after disclosure violates GDPR requirements for transparency and data subject rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 14 GDPR – Information to be provided where personal data have not been obtained from the data subject - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where personal data have not been obtained from the data subject, the controller shall provide the data subject with the following information: the identity and the contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller’s representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; the categories of personal data concerned; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a recipient in a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47 , or the second subparagraph of Article 49 (1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition to the information referred to in paragraph 1, the controller shall provide the data subject with the following information necessary to ensure fair and transparent processing in respect of the data subject: the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6 (1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject and to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where processing is based on point (a) of Articl
perturbation.justification: The modification of the contract text, which changes the notification of disclosure from 'in advance of the required disclosure' to 'after the required disclosure,' directly contradicts the intent and requirements of data protection laws. The Law Explanation states that Article 14(5) of GDPR requires informing the data subject of the disclosure, and the modification prevents compliance before the disclosure. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that many data protection laws require notification and an opportunity to object before disclosure, especially under GDPR. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Article 14 of GDPR, discusses the information that must be provided where personal data have not been obtained from the data subject, supporting the need for pre-disclosure notification to ensure fair and transparent processing. The modification undermines the ability of the Disclosing Party to take steps to comply with GDPR, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Each Party as Receiving Party expressly agrees and accepts that except in the case of fraud, no representation or warranty, express or implied, is made by the Disclosing Party as to th e accuracy, completeness, reasonableness or otherwise in respect of the use of the Information, and that neither the Disclosing Party or any of its affiliates nor any of its or their respective employees, officers, secondees, agents, consultants, sub -contr actors and professional advisers (as applicable) shall have any liability to the Receiving Party as a result of the Receiving Party’s possession or use of the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Each Party as Receiving Party expressly agrees and accepts that no representation or warranty, express or implied, is made by the Disclosing Party as to th e accuracy, completeness, reasonableness or otherwise in respect of the use of the Information, and that neither the Disclosing Party or any of its affiliates nor any of its or their respective employees, officers, secondees, agents, consultants, sub -contr actors and professional advisers (as applicable) shall have any liability to the Receiving Party as a result of the Receiving Party’s possession or use of the Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'except in the case of fraud' clause attempts to waive liability even for fraudulent misrepresentation, which is generally unenforceable as against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles against waiving liability for fraud.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195(2)(b) suggests that an agreement exempting a party from liability for fraud is unenforceable. This modification attempts to waive such liability, which would be against public policy and deemed unenforceable in most jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'except in the case of fraud' clause, attempting to waive liability even for fraudulent misrepresentation. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195(2)(b) suggests that an agreement exempting a party from liability for fraud is unenforceable. This is consistent with the Perturbation Explanation, which notes that waiving liability for fraud is generally unenforceable as against public policy. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the removal of the fraud exception directly contradicts the principle that one cannot contract out of liability for fraudulent acts.

perturbation.original_text: 21. This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by English law and the Parties hereby submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by English law, except that the Receiving Party agrees to resolve all disputes with the Disclosing Party through binding arbitration in a jurisdiction of the Disclosing Party's choosing. The Parties hereby submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a mandatory binding arbitration clause, particularly one that allows the Disclosing Party to choose the jurisdiction, could be seen as unconscionable if it effectively deprives the Receiving Party of a fair and impartial hearing, conflicting with fundamental principles of due process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) & Principles of Unconscionability
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration, clauses that are unconscionable (e.g., heavily one-sided, deprive a party of meaningful redress) are unenforceable. The modification, by allowing the Disclosing Party to choose the arbitration jurisdiction, introduces a potential element of unconscionability that could render the arbitration agreement unenforceable, violating basic principles of contract law and fairness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces mandatory binding arbitration with the Disclosing Party choosing the jurisdiction. This could be deemed unconscionable and deprive the Receiving Party of a fair hearing. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that arbitration clauses can be unenforceable if they are heavily one-sided and deprive a party of meaningful redress. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 9 U.S. Code § 2, indicates that arbitration agreements are valid and enforceable, "save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract." The added clause, allowing the Disclosing Party to choose the jurisdiction, could be grounds for revocation based on unconscionability, thus contradicting the general enforceability of arbitration agreements under the FAA.
